html:
RECOUP Integrative, Root-Cause Primary Care Program

Current healthcare options

Self-paying patients visit either a "family doctor" or a specialist based on Google or recommendations from friends, family, or their employer.

Run-of-the-mill "family doctors"

  • Treat patients symptomatically (e.g., pain killers, antibiotics, antacids, antidepressants or corticosteroids) without addressing the root causes and preventing recurrence. The patient stops follow-up once there is slight relief and keeps self-medicating continuously or intermittently, often lifelong, with life-threatening consequences.
  • Chronic diseases like diabetes, hypertension, hypothyroidism, depression, gastroesophageal reflux or arthritis are typically treated with medicines for a lifetime.
  • Address one symptom at a time, failing to recognize the interconnectedness of different body systems and physiological imbalances, e.g., the link between poor gut health and depression.
  • Primary care physicians are often untrained to provide evidence-based care consistent with current guidelines. Many "general practitioners and family doctors" in India have no medical qualifications whatsoever. Of course, a few accredited family medicine programs do exist.
  • The assessment and follow-up are subjective, lacking prognostic scores, risk stratification, biometric assessments, AI-based decision support systems, and digital therapeutics.
  • Inefficient systems design, inadequate IT systems, complex referral processes, and a lack of patient-centred approaches can lead to errors, diminished quality of care, and patient dissatisfaction.
  • Lack of health coaching services to facilitate behavioral modifications and chronic disease support.
  • For most conditions, except a few trivial ones, the patient is referred out of the facility for further management. Unethical and commercial considerations often drive these referrals.
  • There is no transition between primary and secondary/tertiary care. The patient starts all over again, including repeating recent tests.
  • This approach is expensive, inconvenient, time-consuming and ineffective.

Due to these limitations, most patients seek medical specialists and superspecialists upfront for every health condition. This leads to:

  • Longer wait times for appointments.
  • Need to travel to multiple distant locations.
  • Higher costs of consultations and treatment.
  • Unnecessary and expensive tests and investigations in the pursuit of a specific diagnosis.
  • Potential for overlooking common conditions due to absence of a whole-person approach: Super-specialization can lead to a focus on a specific area of expertise, potentially causing specialists to miss or underestimate common illnesses or comorbidities.
  • There is a risk of over-reliance on technology and a potential disregard for the patient's overall well-being: "Your scan is normal; hence nothing is wrong with you."
  • One-size-fits-all: Despite each patient's unique history, lifestyle, and needs, specialists often lack the time or inclination to listen and customize treatments, leading them to provide generalized care instead.
  • A fragmented healthcare experience exists. Patients often need to visit multiple specialists before receiving a comprehensive diagnosis, leading to "doctor-shopping" and seeking "second opinions" to find the right specialist. This process can be frustrating and time-consuming, potentially worsening their condition. Typically, they visit each specialist only once or a few times, resulting in a transactional encounter that lacks the potential for effective long-term management or prevention of chronic diseases.
  • Healthcare in silos, without communication or coordination among providers, the patient often becomes the messenger between various providers who engage in one-upmanship.
  • This approach is expensive, inconvenient, time-consuming and ineffective.

The high cost of medicines, procedures, and insurance premiums, combined with the high rate of insurance denials, is forcing patients to seek alternatives for their medical problems. Additionally, the realization that conventional medicine is limited in addressing many chronic ailments has instigated a movement toward self-care through internet resources, social media influencers, support groups, and do-it-yourself treatments, often with disastrous consequences. A complementary or alternative medicine practitioner typically asks the patient to discontinue all previous therapies before starting their own, often asserting that their method is vastly superior to others for good measure.

The result of the broken healthcare system is increased morbidity and mortality at a young age, decreased productivity and quality of life, growing patient dissatisfaction, and burnout among healthcare providers.

Only 15-20% of the healing of an individual or a population comes from health care. The remaining—nearly 80%—comes from factors often overlooked by the conventional healthcare system: the behavioral and lifestyle choices individuals make daily, including those related to food, movement, sleep, stress, and substance use. Additionally, economic and social determinants of health increasingly influence these behaviors and significantly impact health and lifespan, often more than physiology or genetics.

What Is Integrative Medicine?

Integrative health involves the optimal combination of all evidence-based approaches to help heal the person as a whole. It is the appropriate merger and integration of conventional care, drugless approaches, including complementary and alternative medicine (CAM), dietary interventions, nutritional supplements, natural medicine, behavior and lifestyle medicine. It may encompass Ayurveda, yoga, naturopathy, tai chi, and traditional Chinese medicine (TCM). Conventional medicines and remedies are also utilized, when necessary, providing a more holistic approach that is well-equipped to address the challenges of chronic conditions.

What Is Root-Cause Medicine?

Root-cause or Functional medicine is an approach to healthcare that aims to identify the underlying root cause of illness, rather than just treating symptoms after they appear. This approach is based on the understanding that all body systems are interconnected, with a particular emphasis on the functional relationship among the digestive, hormonal, and immune systems. Biomedical medicine integrates additional information such as hereditary factors, environmental toxicity, and unique biochemical imbalances like methylation defects, metabolic enzyme problems, and neurochemical dysfunction. Upstream medicine, or Medicine 3.0, focuses on preventive and proactive measures, understanding the social determinants of health, and leveraging technology to track health data, identify risk factors, and personalize interventions.

The functional medicine model is an individualized, patient-centred, science-based approach that empowers patients and practitioners to work together to address the underlying causes of disease and promote optimal wellness. It requires a detailed understanding of each patient's genetic, biochemical, and lifestyle factors and leverages that data to direct personalized treatment plans that lead to improved patient outcomes (IFM).

The root causes of chronic diseases are often masked with:

  • Pharmaceuticals
  • Hormone replacement therapy
  • Supplements
  • Excess caffeine
  • Denial of body signals
  • Only treating symptoms
  • Ignoring emotional trauma and psychological factors
  • Activities leading to a Dopamine rush
  • Turning away from the source

Who is a Whole Person?

A person is more than just their physical components or parts. They are complex, dynamic, interactive systems. Besides the body and the external environment, they are made up of

  1. Their behavior and lifestyle that interacts with the environment,
  2. Their social and emotional dimensions, and
  3. Their mental and spiritual dimensions, which bring meaning and purpose to life.

What is Primary Care?

Primary care is a person's first contact with the health system when they have a health problem or issue that is not an emergency.

The five core functions of primary care are

  1. First contact accessibility guarantees timely and equitable access.
  2. Continuity builds trusted relationships.
  3. Coordination supports the integration of services.
  4. Comprehensiveness ensures access to a full range of services.
  5. People-centeredness empowers people to take an active role in their health.

Primary care is a fundamental aspect of a health system that offers promotive, protective, preventive, curative, rehabilitative, and palliative services throughout life.

RECOUP Integrative, Root-Cause Primary Care Program

Who is this program for?

Patients who are

  1. Recovering from an acute illness or injury
  2. Managing a chronic condition
  3. Looking to feel healthier
  4. Seeking preventive health check-ups
  5. Pursuing performance enhancement in their profession, sports or vocation
  6. Wellbeing enthusiasts and longevity seekers, who take a proactive approach to cultivating optimal life span and healthy ageing and are invested in making healthy lifestyle changes
  7. Biohackers who seek to understand and leverage their bodies' interconnected processes, including their genes, microbiomes, and environments, under professional guidance.

The program suits all age groups, including children, adolescents, adults and the elderly.

The RECOUP program combines conventional and complementary approaches to address the underlying causes of health concerns, promoting holistic well-being. It emphasizes understanding the interconnectedness of physical, mental, emotional, and spiritual needs, aiming for sustainable health and wellness rather than temporarily managing symptoms.

The RECOUP Program has the following characteristics:

  • Advanced primary care services for the entire family: Convenient, expert first contact, comprehensive, coordinated and continuous care for acute illnesses (excluding medical emergencies) and chronic disease management. The family will receive dedicated, compassionate care from our interdisciplinary team tailored to each member's age, health, unique needs, and health goals.
  • Seamless transition to specialist services in the same facility.
  • Integrative Root-Cause Rehabilitation (IRR): We treat every illness using our unique 4R approach – Relief, Root Cause Reversal, Rebalance, and Regeneration, ensuring patients enjoy lasting health. IRR is a cutting-edge modern medicine approach that is physician-led and physician-directed. It is NOT alternative medicine.
  • This collaborative, interdisciplinary approach involves primary care physicians, specialist physicians, and other healthcare professionals from various disciplines working to create health rather than just treat disease.
    • The team conducts thorough assessments using proprietary digital tools and group consultations to create comprehensive, holistic care plans, ensuring patients receive personalized and coordinated treatment.
    • They align with the patient's diagnosis, therapeutic goals, and treatment plans, prioritize and stage various therapies, and follow up on their efficacy.
    • The primary care physician follows up for most conditions, and the specialists are involved only if needed.
    • IRR's focus on promoting patient self-management and behavioral modification through health coaches can also reduce the risk of dependence on long-term medications, providing sustainable alternatives to conventional management.
  • Proprietary Anamnestic Assessments: Anamnesis is the process of gathering a patient's medical history, which includes past illnesses, symptoms, and current living conditions. The physician organizes the patient's root causes of disease and physiological imbalances using the functional medicine timeline and matrix, a framework based on systems biology that helps identify patterns and triggers. Specialized questionnaires for physiological, structural, mental, or emotional dysfunction are used. Functional Lab tests, genetic tests, and analytes/biomarkers offer insights into digestion, gut dysbiosis, food sensitivities, hormones, immunity, neurotransmitters, toxicity, micronutrient deficiencies, and more.
  • RECOUP Entire Person Health Score (EPHS) and Care Plan Generation using artificial intelligence and machine learning, including:
    • EPHS S (Subjective),
    • EPHS O (Objective).
    • EPHS P (Physiological)
    • Entire Person Mental Health Score (EPMHS)
  • Use cutting-edge biometrics and digital technology for assessment, including body composition analysis, medical infrared thermography, heart rate variability, cardiometabolic analyzer, insulin resistance analyzer, pedobarography, instrumented gait and posture analysis.
  • Rehabilitation is "a set of interventions designed to optimize functioning and reduce disability in individuals with health conditions in interaction with their environment" (WHO). For example, physiotherapists use specialized manual therapy (Dermoneuromodulation), biofeedback, mindful movement (Tai Chi, Qi Gong, Neurocognitive Imagery or Franklin method), vagus nerve stimulation, aquatic therapy, and other techniques to improve mobility, reduce pain, and improve strength and endurance.
  • Adaptogenic processes refer to the body's ability to adapt and respond to stress by maintaining or restoring balance and increasing resistance to various stressors.
  • Salutogenesis, which means "the creation of health," is a framework that focuses on understanding the origins of health and well-being, rather than solely on disease and its causes. It emphasizes the factors contributing to a person's ability to maintain and enhance their overall health and resilience, especially in adversity.
  • Whole person care requires meticulous attention to all factors influencing health, wellness, and disease, including the body, mind, spirit, and community. These factors may include lifestyle choices, work and home environments, nutrition, interpersonal relationships, physical activity, and outlook on life.
  • Root-cause and Functional Medicine provide system-oriented evaluations to identify and address the root causes of health issues. They view the body as an interconnected system in which an imbalance in one area can negatively affect other areas, including genes, environment, diet, and lifestyle.
  • Holistic and Integrative Approach: Physicians of modern medicine (allopathy) team up with Ayurveda, TCM, Yoga and Naturopathy physicians to provide the best elements from all these approaches, emphasizing research and evidence-based practices. All the team members are equally trained and speak the same medical language.
  • Functional nutrition is a holistic approach to diet and lifestyle that emphasizes food's therapeutic potential in supporting and optimizing bodily functions. It goes beyond basic nutrition by considering the interconnectedness of various systems within the body and how food choices and lifestyle factors affect them. Functional nutritionists aim to address the root causes of health issues by creating personalized nutrition plans that restore balance and support the body's natural healing processes.
  • Integrative root-cause psychology is a holistic approach that combines different psychological perspectives and techniques to understand and address the underlying causes of mental health issues.
  • Preventive care includes:
    • Annual health check-ups using RECOUP EPHS and biometrics,
    • Pre-conception clinic for medical care and advice provided to individuals or couples before they become pregnant, aiming to enhance health and well-being to optimize pregnancy outcomes and the future child's health,
    • Well-child visits,
    • Insurance-related check-ups,
    • Health promotion through group visits, newsletters and webinars,
    • Travel medicine,
    • Vaccination,
    • Ergonomic and environmental assessment of the home and workplace,
    • Fitness for employment (including functional capacity evaluation) and return to work.
  • Personalized care: This involves identifying tailored interventions based on each patient's genetic profile while considering other factors such as patients' abilities, contexts, needs, social conditions, lifestyles, family histories, concomitant therapies, and psychological aspects.
  • Precision medicine: To classify patients with a disease into subgroups, considering their phenotypic findings, such as biomarkers or genomics. Genomics offers a powerful tool for improving health outcomes by enabling earlier diagnosis, precision treatment, and targeted prevention. Nutrigenomics, the study of how genes and nutrition interact, has the potential to personalize dietary recommendations for specific conditions or prevent diseases.
  • Emphasis on Lifestyle Factors: Recognizes the significance of whole-food, plant-predominant nutrition, physical activity, restorative sleep, stress management, avoidance of risky substances, and positive social connections in preventing, managing, and even reversing chronic diseases, and promoting overall health and well-being.

What makes the RECOUP program different?

The physician-led, interdisciplinary team:

  • It is patient-centred, relationship-based, and recovery-focused. It encompasses practitioner communication styles (listening, understanding, explaining, validating, empathy, intent), patient-practitioner partnerships (shared decision-making and treatment planning), sufficient visit time, and patient trust.
  • Listens to the patient and attempts to understand their unique story, needs, goals, culture, beliefs and values. Understanding the reasons for the visit and their information needs.
  • Meets the patient where they are and works on shared decisions for their care.
  • Tailors a bespoke care plan that leads to action, empowerment, and positive expectation and supports them in achieving their goals.
  • Creates a continuing partnership (therapeutic alliance) in which patients actively share decision-making and responsibility for their health.
  • Provides health coaching, motivational interviewing, and behavioral modification to handhold the patient, enhance self-efficacy, identify challenges, monitor progress and provide follow-up.
  • Utilizes artificial intelligence, machine learning and biometric technology for deep assessment, creates personalized care plans, and sequentially tracks progress objectively. For example, medical infrared thermography can non-invasively document changes in inflammation in a body region every few days until it resolves.
  • Improved patient outcomes, satisfaction and quality of care by incorporating a wider range of evidence-based therapies and lifestyle interventions.
  • Lower costs: By reducing the need for repeated interventions and medication.
  • A comprehensive in-house inventory of supplements, nutraceuticals, botanicals, fitness, ergonomics and wellness products. Several supplements and medications are exclusively sourced through a compounding pharmacy.
  • Access to basic and specialized lab testing.
  • Addresses the social determinants of health to the extent possible, including the conditions in which people are born, live, work, play, and age, shaping their health outcomes.
  • It combines the benefits of conventional and complementary management to reduce dependency on prolonged polypharmacy.
  • The approach is inquiry-driven and open to new paradigms and continuous improvement.

Research has shown that this advanced primary care improves outcomes, lowers costs, and satisfies patients and healthcare providers. The physician cares
for the patient throughout that patient's entire life cycle and provides all evidence-based services needed to prevent and treat common diseases. Evidence shows that integrating behavioral health services with lifestyle change support can significantly improve or reverse many chronic diseases prevalent in primary care, including obesity, diabetes, hypertension, cardiovascular disease, depression, and substance abuse.

The RECOUP Interdisciplinary Team

Health Ingredient Health Professional
Lifestyle modificationLifestyle and Functional Medicine Physician
Improved functionRehabilitation Physician, Occupational Therapist, Physiotherapist, Speech Therapist, Ergonomist
NutritionNutritionist

Behavior changesClinical Psychologist, health coach
Removing barriersCase manager, health coach
Therapeutic touchManual physiotherapist, Ayurveda therapist, Aquatic therapist

Conditions treated

The following conditions are suitable for this approach:

  • Musculoskeletal Pain and Disorders, including neck and back pain, arthritis, fibromyalgia, pelvic pain, temporomandibular disorders, headaches, tendinopathies, osteoporosis
  • Neuropathic pain and compressive neuropathies
  • Metabolism: energy dysregulation, chronic fatigue, etc.
  • Women's health: infertility, preconception, pre and post-natal care, Premenstrual syndrome, PCOS, menopause.
  • Men's health: benign prostatic hyperplasia, erectile dysfunction, male infertility, andropause, prostate and testicular cancer.
  • Hormone Imbalance: hypo and hyperthyroidism, Hashimoto's Thyroiditis, Dysfunction of the Adrenal, Male and Female Sex Hormones
  • Mental Health: Depression, Anxiety, Stress, Emotional Trauma
  • Cognitive Health: Altered Mental Focus and Stamina, Prevention of Cognitive Decline
  • Neurodegenerative disorders: Parkinson's, Multiple Sclerosis, Dementia, etc.
  • Cardiometabolic disorders: diabetes, hypertension, insulin resistance, dyslipidemia, high uric acid, cardiovascular, cerebrovascular and peripheral vascular disorders
  • Metabolic dysfunction-associated steatotic liver disease (MASLD)
  • Immune disorders: autoimmunity, chronic inflammation, neuroinflammation
  • Sinus and pulmonary conditions, e.g. asthma, recurrent respiratory infections
  • Environmental conditions: Mold and toxicity from chemicals and chronic infections
  • Sleep Disturbances
  • Addictions and Dependencies – Smoking and alcohol cessation programs
  • Nutritional deficiencies and disorders
  • Obesity and weight gain
  • Gastrointestinal Issues – Achlorhydria, GERD, bloating, irritable bowel syndrome, inflammatory bowel disease, SIBO, Intestinal permeability, constipation, food allergies, intolerances, and sensitivities
  • Cancer prevention and supportive management (metabolic terrain)
  • Skin health
  • Ehlers-Danlos Syndrome and Hypermobility Spectrum Disorders
  • Spinal deformities – scoliosis, kyphosis
  • Long Covid
  • Sports and Performing Arts Medicine
  • Childhood neurodiverse conditions: Autism Spectrum Disorder, ADHD, PANS, PANDAS
  • Cerebral palsy and developmental delay
  • Pediatric orthopedic conditions – DDH, limb deformities, arthrogryposis, spina bifida, etc.

Unique treatment modalities at RECOUP

  • Vagus nerve stimulation (neuromodulation) and polyvagal approaches
  • Heart rate variability, neuro and EMG biofeedback
  • Pulsed Electromagnetic Field Therapy
  • Intravenous therapy
  • Peptide therapy
  • Bioidentical hormone therapy
  • Regenerative therapies – prolotherapy, platelet-rich plasma, perineural injections, stem cell therapy, Gold Induced Cytokines (GOLDIC) therapy, Bone Marrow Aspirate Concentrate (BMAC) therapy
  • Medical ozone therapy – local, intra-articular, MAHT
  • Physiological pain portals
  • Aquatic therapy in a temperature-controlled swimming pool
  • Colon hydrotherapy, coffee enema
  • Detoxification therapies
  • Far Infrared sauna and red-light therapy
  • Coordination dynamics therapy
  • Augmented reality-based therapy
  • Exergaming
  • Hippotherapy
  • Whole body vibration therapy
  • Body weight supported treadmill training
  • Interventional pain management
  • Entire range of physiotherapy, occupational therapy, speech therapy, orthotics
